Battle of Umberkhind

[Maratha History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/maratha-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Battle of Umberkhind

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 03, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

4

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

Share

Around 4 km from Khemabari village in the Khalapur taluk of Raigad district, on the Khapoli-Poli road, is located a small tri junction at Chavani. The Amba river here originates from the foot of a small cone, near to Kurnawade village in the Maval Taluka. The river passes by Umarkhandi along the Garmal valley, and merging with the Nagothane creek, before it merges into the sea.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jM5U!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8057722b-4e22-49eb-9992-dec9ce01cb63_300x224.jpeg)

![umb1](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!jM5U!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8057722b-4e22-49eb-9992-dec9ce01cb63_300x224.jpeg "umb1")

Umberkhind is one of the many ghats in the Nain-Maval area, located right below Kurnawade Ghat. During the first Anglo Maratha War in 1781, the Peshwa chieftain Haripant Phadke, guarded this area to prevent the British from approaching Ghatmatha via Kuranwade Ghat, instead of Bor Ghat. One of the narrowest passes, in the Western Ghats, separating Lohghad from Visaghad running parallel to Mumbai-Pune rail route, it is a popular trekking route in the Sahyadris.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sr-o!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff232652e-c297-41b2-bfb9-b3b196a1d4d4_284x177.jpeg)

![umb](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!sr-o!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Ff232652e-c297-41b2-bfb9-b3b196a1d4d4_284x177.jpeg "umb")

This pass would however be remembered for a historic battle fought here on February 3, 1661, when Chattrapati Shivaji Maharaj defeated Kartalab Khan, the right hand man of Shaishta Khan, one of the most decisive victories ever, that further consolidated his position and strengthened the Marathas.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gQVC!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a038c96-ce11-4c0d-8884-7bb029d815e1_300x165.jpeg)

![shiv](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gQVC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a038c96-ce11-4c0d-8884-7bb029d815e1_300x165.jpeg "shiv")

Kartalab Khan wasn’t just any Mughal commande, he was Shaista Khan’s trusted lieutenant, handpicked by Aurangzeb to break Shivaji’s rising power in the Deccan. After establishing Mughal headquarters at Pune, he had an early success capturing Chakan Fort in 1660.

Around 1661, Shaista Khan ordered him to capture the crucial forts of **K**alyan, Bhiwandi, Panvel, Chul, and Nagothane, which controlled access to the northern Konkan coast, vital for trade and military movement. The force included Rajput chieftains like Chaturbuj Chauhan, Amarsingh Chandravat, Mitrasen and his brother Sarjerao Gadhe.

Kartalab Khan started from Pune on February 3, 1661, leading an army of 20,000 troops, which while was adept at open plains warfare, had no experience dealing with guerilla warfare which the Marathas used. While he was skilled in open plains warfare, honed in the North against mercenary armies, he was not familiar with the dense forests, narrow passes, and steep ridges of the Sahyadris.

On the other hand the Marathas knew the terrain in and out, experts at ambushes,sudden strikes, and mobility that made them invisible until the moment of attack. Above all, they were driven the vision of Hindavi Swarajya, they were not some rag tag bunch of mercenaries.

Kartalab made his way via Chinchwad, Talegaon, Vadagon, and instead of taking a turn towards Lohagad fort, that was right on the border between the Sahayadris and Konkan, he took the steeper descent from Ghathmatha towards Umberkhind, a very narrow route. Bhor Ghat was **wider, more open, and strategically safer** for a large army and had he taken that route, would have been tougher for Shivaji Maharaj.

One of the main factors in Shivaji Maharaj’s success was his intelligence network. He placed informants inside Mughal and Adilshahi garrisons, who reported troop movements, supply lines, and morale. Villagers, traders, and even shepherds acted as eyes and ears, feeding Raja real-time updates about enemy movements through the ghats and valleys.

Messages were carried by trusted couriers across the Sahyadris, often faster than the enemy could march. Shivaji Maharaj was known to plant agents who misled enemy generals with false information, steering them into traps like Umberkhind.

His spies deliberately spread word that he was at Pen (Raigad district), making it seem like the Marathas were concentrated elsewhere. And this was why Kartalab abandoned his safer plan of marching via Bor Ghat (Khandala Ghat, a wide, open route where his 20,000 troops could maneuver. He instead descended into Umberkhind Pass, thinking Shivaji Maharaj was far away, when in reality he was waiting with his forces hidden in the forests and cliffs.

To put it into perspective Umbarkhind is 2200 feet above sea level, you would need a descent of 300 feet to Tugaranya, which was surrounded on all sides by forest and hills. The geography created a bowl where Kartalab Khan’s army was exposed from all sides, unable to maneuver.

Kartalab Khan’s army, trained for open plains, was funneled into a narrow gorge where cavalry charges and artillery were useless. Shivaji Maharaj grew up in the Sahyadris and Western Ghats, he knew every pass, every hidden trail, every vantage point. This was akin to bearding the lion in his own den.

Shivaji Maharaj and his 1000 strong men were hiding in wait, equipped with arrows, boulders, rifles, ready to rain down from the cliffs. They were well concealed by the dense foliage, and Maharaj himself, was waiting mounted on horseback, bow in one hand, spear in the other, sword at his waist. As Kartalab Khan’s 20,000-strong army descended into the narrow gorge, the Marathas closed in silently from all sides. By the time they reached the base at Tugaranya, they were surrounded in a bowl of hills and forests.

The Marathas rolled **boulders** down the slopes, smashing into the Mughal columns trapped in the narrow gorge.Thanks to the **thick forest cover**, the Mughal soldiers couldn’t even see where the assault was coming from. Panic spread quickly. By the time Kartalab Khan’s men realized what was happening, they were already **surrounded on all sides**.

The Marathas then descended, unleashing **arrows, rifle fire, spears, and swords**, pouncing on the disoriented Mughal troops.The entire engagement lasted barely **2–3 hours**, as the 20,000 strong Mughal army was routed by 1000 of Shivaji’s men. Kartalab’s prestige was shattered, and his Rajput allies too were humbled.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7U_v!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcdb1fcb0-3500-4313-8fc1-11ed0f77e237_300x168.jpeg)

![shivk](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7U_v!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fcdb1fcb0-3500-4313-8fc1-11ed0f77e237_300x168.jpeg "shivk")

Realizing his army was trapped and annihilation was imminent, Kartalab sent word to Shivaji Maharaj, offering to **surrender and retreat**. Maharaj accepted, showing his characteristic balance, he knew when to strike hard and when to consolidate. Kartalab Khan and **Mitrasen** personally laid their weapons before Shivaji, who ordered all Maratha sardars to halt the attack, allowing the Mughal-Rajput forces to withdraw.

Kartalab Khan’s army pulled back, humiliated and broken, as they abandoned their **weapons, supplies, and artillery**, which fell into Maratha hands. This windfall strengthened Shivaji’s arsenal, giving the Marathas more resources for future campaigns. They also left behind **large amounts of gold, jewelry, weapons, and supplies**, giving the Marathas a massive boost in firepower and resources. Also some Mughal soldiers too switched sides, and Netaji Palkar, one of Maharaj’s most trusted generals was appointed in charge of the Konkan, to **thwart further Mughal attacks**, consolidate Maratha control, and secure the newly gained wealth and territory. Palkar in turn ensured that the Konkan became a stronghold, buffering Shivaji Maharaj’s kingdom against future Mughal attacks.

Once again a 1000 strong Maratha Army under Shivaji Maharaj had defeated a much larger Mughal force of around 20,000, just using smart strategy and tactics. The victory at Umberkhind is considered one of the great victories in Shivaji Maharaj’s career, and is commemorated every year on February 3 since 2001.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YbGl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa315bfd2-6803-429d-9f5e-0a72dc3a9af6_194x259.jpeg)

![umb](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YbGl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa315bfd2-6803-429d-9f5e-0a72dc3a9af6_194x259.jpeg "umb")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Shiva Mitra, a Lonavala based organization has erected a victory memorial here that was inaugurated in 2007 by Babasaheb Purandhare and Udayan Raje Bhonsle. The memorial is around 12.5 m high, bearing a statue of Shivaji Maharaj in the front. A brief history of Umberkhind battle is engraved on the right side of the memorial.
